text.title: Database Design Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Schema mistakes: god tables, missing FKs, over-indexing, EAV abuse, FLOAT for money, CASCADE everywhere.

antipattern.description: God table: a single table with 50+ columns for loosely related entities.
antipattern.reason: Makes every query wide; most columns are NULL for any given row; adding indexes for different query patterns bloats write overhead. Normalize into focused tables and join when needed.

antipattern.description: EAV (Entity-Attribute-Value) pattern for structured data with a known schema.
antipattern.reason: EAV destroys type safety, makes queries unreadable, eliminates index effectiveness, and prevents constraint enforcement. Use JSONB for truly dynamic attributes; use proper columns for known fields.

antipattern.description: Relying on application-layer foreign key enforcement without database-level constraints.
antipattern.reason: Application code is bypassed by migrations, bulk loads, direct DB access, and bugs. Database-level FK constraints are the only reliable enforcement.

antipattern.description: Using FLOAT or DOUBLE for monetary amounts.
antipattern.reason: IEEE 754 floating-point cannot represent most decimal fractions exactly. Repeated arithmetic accumulates rounding errors. Always use NUMERIC(12,2) or BIGINT cents for money.

antipattern.description: Adding an index to every column "for performance" without measuring query patterns first.
antipattern.reason: Each index adds overhead to every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE. A table with 20 indexes takes 20x write overhead compared to a table with 1 index. Index only columns that appear in WHERE, JOIN, or ORDER BY clauses of frequent queries.

antipattern.description: Adding a NOT NULL column with a DEFAULT value in a single ALTER TABLE on a large production table.
antipattern.reason: In Postgres, this rewrites the entire table and holds an exclusive lock for minutes to hours on large tables. Use the expand-contract pattern: add nullable, backfill in batches, add NOT NULL constraint separately.

antipattern.description: Applying ON DELETE CASCADE to foreign keys without careful review of the deletion chain.
antipattern.reason: A cascade chain 3+ tables deep means a single parent delete wipes all descendant rows. A developer deleting a test user can wipe all their orders, payments, and shipments in a single query.
